summary: American poet, short story writer, and satirist. Founding member of the Algonquin Round Table. Best known for the poem "Resume" ("Razors pain you... you might as well live"), "News Item" ("Men seldom make passes / At girls who wear glasses"), the short story "Big Blonde" (Hazel Morse), and her caustic "Constant Reader" book reviews for The New Yorker.

works.name: Resume
works.description: Eight-line poem cataloging the downsides of various suicide methods, concluding "you might as well live."

clues.clue: Concludes with "You might as well live"
examples: a poem that ends by proclaiming 'Gas smells awful; You might as well live'
concluding 'You might as well live'

clues.clue: Includes lines "Acids stain you," "Guns aren't lawful; / Nooses give"
examples: notes 'Acids stain you' and 'Gas smells awful'
Nooses give, / Gas smells awful. / You might as well live

works.name: Big Blonde
works.description: Short story about Hazel Morse, an alcoholic woman who fails to kill herself with veronal. Won the 1929 O. Henry Award.

clues.clue: Protagonist Hazel Morse — struggles with alcoholism and depression
examples: Hazel Morse struggles with alcoholism and depression at Jimmy's bar
the alcoholic Hazel Morse survives an overdose

clues.clue: Hazel tries to kill herself with twenty tablets of veronal but fails; wakes two days later and shares a drink with maid Nettie
examples: tries to kill herself with twenty tablets of Veronal; after waking up two days later, she shares a drink with her maid, Nettie
Hazel Morse survives an overdose of veronal

works.name: Love Song
works.description: Poem opening "My own dear love, he is strong and bold" and ending "I wish somebody'd shoot him."

clues.clue: Opens "My own dear love, he is strong and bold" — each stanza ends with a line like "I wish somebody'd shoot him"
examples: opened a poem with the lines 'My own dear love, he is strong and bold / And he cares not what comes after.' That poem, which ends 'I wish somebody'd shoot him'
three stanzas extolling the virtues of her lover, each stanza ending with a line like 'And I wish I'd never met him' or 'And I wish somebody'd shoot him'

works.name: Inventory
works.description: Poem from the collection Enough Rope. Lists "four of the things I'd rather be without: Love, curiosity, freckles, and doubt."

clues.clue: Lists "Love, curiosity, freckles, and doubt" as things she'd rather be without; also lists "envy, content, and sufficient champagne" as unattainable
examples: catalogs 'Love, curiosity, freckles, and doubt' as 'Four of the things I'd rather be without'
three be the things I shall never attain: envy, content, and sufficient champagne

works.name: One Perfect Rose
works.description: Poem in which the speaker receives "one perfect rose" but wishes someone would send "one perfect limousine."

clues.clue: Speaker asks "Why is it no one ever sent me yet / One perfect limousine" — lamenting it's always her luck to get "one perfect rose"
examples: asks 'Why is it no one ever sent me yet / One perfect limousine, do you suppose?' before lamenting that it's always her luck to get 'one perfect rose'

works.name: The Standard of Living
works.description: Short story about two stenographers, Annabel and Midge, who play a game of "what would you do with a million dollars."

clues.clue: Two stenographers Annabel and Midge argue over silver-fox coats and ask for the price of a string of pearls
examples: two 'conspicuous and cheap and charming' stenographers who 'lunch on sugar' argue over whether silver-fox coats are 'common.' Annabel and Midge ask for the price of a string of pearls

works.name: The Immortals
works.description: Poem about two lovers who deceive a "mooning world" but are really enemies — compared to Abelard and Heloise.

clues.clue: Two lovers deceive a "mooning world" with "how prettily we sigh and swear" — compared to Abelard and Heloise
examples: describes herself and a lover who deceive a 'mooning world' with 'how prettily we sigh and swear,' saying 'And though we lie forever enemies, / Shall rank with Abelard and Heloise'

works.name: A Telephone Call
works.description: Short story consisting entirely of the thoughts of a woman waiting for a man to call her.

clues.clue: Entire story is the thoughts of a woman waiting for a man to call — he calls her "darling" twice

A woman waits for a call from a man who calls her 'darling' twice

comprehensive_summary:
Dorothy Parker is an American poet, short story writer, and satirist best known as a founding member of the Algonquin Round Table, the group of 1920s New York wits (also called "the Vicious Circle") that included Robert Benchley, Robert Sherwood, George S. Kaufman, and Alexander Woollcott.

Her most frequently clued poem is "Resume," an eight-line piece that opens "Razors pain you; / Rivers are damp" and catalogs the drawbacks of various suicide methods ("Acids stain you," "Drugs cause cramp," "Guns aren't lawful; / Nooses give," "Gas smells awful") before concluding "You might as well live." Her two-line poem "News Item" is equally iconic: "Men seldom make passes / At girls who wear glasses."

"Big Blonde" is her most important short story, centering on Hazel Morse, a woman called a "good sport" by men who struggles with alcoholism and depression. Hazel's husband Herbie gives her a black eye and leaves for a job in Detroit. She spends nights at Jimmy's bar with boyfriends, toasting "Here's mud in your eye." Her behavior at the theater is a "standing joke" because she cries at everything. She attempts suicide with twenty tablets of veronal but fails, waking two days later to share a drink with her maid Nettie.

Other notable poems include "General Review of the Sex Situation" ("Woman wants monogamy; Man delights in novelty... Count to ten, and man is bored"), "Love Song" (each stanza ends with lines like "I wish somebody'd shoot him"), "Inventory" from the collection Enough Rope ("Love, curiosity, freckles, and doubt"), and "One Perfect Rose" (wishing someone would send "one perfect limousine" instead). Her story "The Standard of Living" follows stenographers Annabel and Midge playing a million-dollar fantasy game. "A Telephone Call" consists entirely of a woman's thoughts while waiting for a man to call.

Parker wrote the "Constant Reader" book review column for The New Yorker, most famously savaging A.A. Milne with the line "Tonstant Weader Fwowed up" in response to his use of the word "hummy." She was on the magazine's first board of editors. She got her start replacing P.G. Wodehouse at Vanity Fair and co-wrote the screenplay for A Star is Born with her husband Alan Campbell. She parodied Housman, imagining "how gay 'twould be / to hang me from a flowering tree," and wrote a response to Marlowe's "Passionate Shepherd" containing "we'll always be Jung together." She was fictionalized as Gora Dwight in Gertrude Atherton's Black Oxen, and her short story "Lolita" about widow Mrs. Ewing was published three weeks before Nabokov's novel of the same name.
